coredata.dc:title: Room-temperature one-step instant synthesis of carbon dots as a new sensing platform for the estimation of hydroxocobalamin in water and food samples

coredata.dc:description: In this research, we synthesized a high-quality fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) at room temperature from the immediate reaction of beta-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate sodium with ethanolamine. The ratio of the substrates to each other was optimized to attain the carbon dots with the highest quantum yield. The optimized prepared dots show greenish-blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm) with a high apparent quantum yield of 36.4 %. The sensor response remained stable for more than two months. Beta-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate sodium and ethanolamine-derived quantum dots (NEQDs) exhibit emission-independent excitation behavior, where all emissions centered at 450 nm after the different setting of excitation wavelengths indicating well-synthesis of mono-fluorophore carbon dots. The fluorescence intensity of NEQDs shows extreme quenching upon the quantitative addition of hydroxocobalamin over a range of 0.30–30.0 μg/mL with a detection limit of 0.10 µg/mL. The mechanism of NEQDs quenching is mainly ascribed to the inner filter effect and static quenching effect. The designed study has been applicable for the assay of hydroxocobalamin in pharmaceutical ampoules, water samples, and food samples, including milk and liver. The recoveries of hydroxocobalamin were 99.15 %–100.98 % in dosage forms and 98.00 %–102.90 % for water and food samples. The sensing results demonstrate the ability of NEQDs to detect hydroxocobalamin unaffected by other vitamins including vitamin B1, biotin (B7), as well as l-carnitine. The greenness of the method was verified through complex-GAPI, AGREE, and AGREE-prep approaches, as well as whiteness assessment and blueness evaluation using the BAGI tool.

head.ce:title: Room-temperature one-step instant synthesis of carbon dots as a new sensing platform for the estimation of hydroxocobalamin in water and food samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, 35516 Mansoura, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, 35516 Mansoura, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this research, we synthesized a high-quality fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) at room temperature from the immediate reaction of beta-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate sodium with ethanolamine. The ratio of the substrates to each other was optimized to attain the carbon dots with the highest quantum yield. The optimized prepared dots show greenish-blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm) with a high apparent quantum yield of 36.4 %. The sensor response remained stable for more than two months. Beta-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate sodium and ethanolamine-derived quantum dots (NEQDs) exhibit emission-independent excitation behavior, where all emissions centered at 450 nm after the different setting of excitation wavelengths indicating well-synthesis of mono-fluorophore carbon dots. The fluorescence intensity of NEQDs shows extreme quenching upon the quantitative addition of hydroxocobalamin over a range of 0.30–30.0 μg/mL with a detection limit of 0.10 µg/mL. The mechanism of NEQDs quenching is mainly ascribed to the inner filter effect and static quenching effect. The designed study has been applicable for the assay of hydroxocobalamin in pharmaceutical ampoules, water samples, and food samples, including milk and liver. The recoveries of hydroxocobalamin were 99.15 %–100.98 % in dosage forms and 98.00 %–102.90 % for water and food samples. The sensing results demonstrate the ability of NEQDs to detect hydroxocobalamin unaffected by other vitamins including vitamin B1, biotin (B7), as well as l-carnitine. The greenness of the method was verified through complex-GAPI, AGREE, and AGREE-prep approaches, as well as whiteness assessment and blueness evaluation using the BAGI tool.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanomaterials are a heterogeneous collection of substances mostly formed by carbon atoms organized in distinct nanoscale configurations. They possess distinct physical and chemical characteristics, making them appropriate for various purposes, including fluorescence sensing[1,2] [Ref], fluorescence imaging [3], and acting as nano extraction beds [4,5]. Carbon dots (CDs) in the field of nano-fluorescence have become more popular due to their outstanding electrical and optical properties, easy functionalization, and promising biocompatibility. They have unique optical features, including tunable fluorescence and emission wavelengths, making them attractive for numerous applications in fields including bio-imaging, sensing, and optoelectronics [6–9]. Their selectivity and sensitivity make them appreciated tools in environmental monitoring and analytical chemistry. Nowadays, a series of cost-effective, simple, and environmentally friendly CD synthesis methods have been developed with the increasing use and applications of CDs. Regarding diverse mechanisms, the composition of CDs is classified into two paths: “top-down” and “bottom-up”. The “top-down” synthesis methods include the reduction of large carbon sources, either chemically or physically, to obtain small CDs. Graphite rods and carbon fibers are utilized as carbon sources in a “top-down” manner. On the other hand, the “bottom-up” synthesis approach uses small carbon sources under ionic or molecular states to fabricate CDs. Different precursors were previously reported for the synthesis of CDs, including red lentils [10], black seed [11], thiosemicarbazide [12], l-histidine [13], citric acid [13], m-phenylenediamine [14], spirulina algae [15,16], and red beetroot [17]. Optimization of CDs occurs during synthesis and post-treatment, as their structure and constitution determine their diverse properties [18,19]. The remarkable water solubility and biocompatibility of CDs are related to the predominant presence of hydrophilic groups on their surfaces, especially hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Moreover, the chemical inertness of CDs allows them to gain wide popularity by confirming compatibility and stability across several fields [20–22]. Specifically, sensors based onnanostructuresprovide a more sensitive detection due to their prominent properties.

Hydroxocobalamin (HC) is a water-soluble vitamin that belongs to the vitamin B family and is not synthesized in the human body. Hence, it should be provided from external vitamin supplements or daily food. It is a complex organometallic compound containing a corrin ring coordinated with a cobalt ion in the center. It is applied for treatingcyanide poisoningthroughsmoke inhalation [23]. Itinhibits nitric oxide-mediatedvasodilation (NO-MV) and has been used in settings of refractoryshock [24]. Its deficiency may cause serious health problems, including ataxia, anemia, neuropathy, gastrointestinal symptoms, and cardiovascular diseases. Excessive intake of HC can also lead to unpredicted adverse effects, including bone marrow dysplasia, kidney failure, and liver disease. Thus, sensing the level of HCin medicine, physiological fluids, environmental waters, and food is of great importance [25]. Several assay methods have been established for the quantitative estimation of HC in various matrices, using spectrophotometry [26,27], HPLC [28–31], and voltammetry [32,33]. Among analytical assay techniques, spectrofluorimetry has numerous advantages, including selectivity, sensitivity, rapid linear response over a wide concentration range, low cost, applicability in different matrices, and easy accessibility compared to previously published values using complex experimental steps [28–31] and techniques [32,33].

In the present work, we seek to prepare much lower-cost and immediate synthesis CDs to replace other CDs prepared from expensive materials that are tedious and time-consuming. Herein, CDs were efficiently synthesized from the direct interaction of beta-naphthoquinone-4-sulfonate sodium (NQS) with ethanolamine (EA) at room temperature. This study aims to design and operate a low-cost and simple yet appropriately sensitive fluorescent probe for the measurement of HC in different matrices, including pharmaceutical forms, food samples, and water samples based on quenching response. Preparation of the optical sensor at room temperature using our developed method results in time-reduction compared to other CDs manufactured by other conventional methods. Additionally, the synthesis occurs at room temperature with zero energy consumption, unlike other reported methods that use hydrothermal and microwave-assisted approaches. Synthesizing CDs at room temperature can take several minutes [34], hours [35–37], and days [38], unlike our method, which takes seconds. In addition, we have avoided using hydrogen peroxide as in the previous methods [34], as it leads to irritation at the site of contact. Inhaling hydrogen peroxide causes irritation of the nose, throat, and respiratory tract. In very severe cases, bronchitis or pulmonary edema [39]. Also, we have avoided the utilization of nitric acid [36,37] and triethylenetetramine [34], which are highly irritating to the skin and eyes and cause skin allergies [40]. Different experimental parameters were studied to prepare the quantum dots with the highest quantum yield. Sensing conditions such as incubation time and pH were optimized to achieve maximum assay sensitivity. Moreover, the characterization and possible quenching mechanisms of the fabricated dots were investigated in detail. Compared with previous works, our designed system indicates a low-cost, simple, time-saving, high-stability, and noticeable sensitivity for HC assay.

Acetic acid 96 %, NQS, EA, quinine sulfate, boric acid, sodium hydroxide, phosphoric acid, glucose, lactose, sodium chloride, sodium sulfite, sodium thiosulfate, sodium sulfate, potassium chloride, lead acetate, ferric chloride, ferrous sulfate, barium chloride, l-cysteine, l-tryptophan, l-lysine, l-histidine, and l-threonine were all obtained from Sigma – Aldrich, Steinheim, Germany. Hydroxocobalamin with 99.10 % purity was kindly supplied by Amriya Pharmaceutical Co., Alexandria, Egypt. Depovit B12® ampoules containing 1000 μg/mL (B.N.8639066) product of Amirya Co. and Depofort B12® ampoules containing 1000 μg/mL (B.N.16230102) product of Pharco Pharmaceutical Co., Alexandria, Egypt, were obtained from local pharmacy. River water samples were obtained from Nile River banks, while sewage water samples were mainly collected from the water assessment unit in Mansoura region, Egypt. Milk and liver samples were bought from a local market in Mansoura , Egypt. All the chemicals/salts were used without purification. Standard solutions of HC and Britton-Robinson buffer solutions were prepared as mentioned in Supplementary section S1.

Fluorescence measurements were accomplished using Cary Eclipse, Santa Clara, CA 95051, USA. Ultraviolet–visible spectra were attained using UV–vis Spectrometer (Shimadzu-1601). Jenway pH-Meter (model 3510, UK) was also utilized. The functional groups on the surface of the prepared dots were identified in the rangeof 400 to 4000 cm−1, adopting a Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectrophotometer (IS10 Nicolet, USA). Scanning electron microscope (SEM, Jeol JSM, 6510LV, Japan) andenergy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX, Oxford-Xmax 20) were conducted to indicate the structure of the dots. The shape of the synthetic material was characterized by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM, JEOL, JEM -2100-Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was collected on K-ALPHA (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) with monochromatic X-ray Al K-alpha radiation −10 to 1350 eV spot size 400 µm at pressure 10−9 mbar with full spectrum pass energy 200 eV and at narrow spectrum 50 eV. Fluorescence lifetime measurements were conducted using a Horiba FluoroCube 3000U-EYM1 fluorescence lifetime measuring system.

The as-prepared NEQDs were synthesized based on Schiff’s base polymerization reaction [41]. According to the previously reported paper [41], carbon dots were prepared from the reaction of hydroquinone and ethylenediamine in the presence of hydrogen peroxide at room temperature. Here, we intended to use NQS instead of hydroquinone and ethanolamine (EA) instead of ethylenediamine, without H2O2, to obtain NEQDs at room temperature as NQS and EA were found to react with each other in an exothermic nature without the need for the addition of hydrogen peroxide. Firstly, 100 mg of NQS was thoroughly dissolved in 6 mL of deionized H2O and exposed to ultra-sonication for 2 min. At room temperature, 2 mL aliquot of ethanolamine was added to the prepared solution of NQS to obtain the immediately brown solution of NEQDs. The resulting pure solution was essentially stored in the refrigerator for subsequent use and characterization. The schematic layout of the synthesis process of the optical sensor and sensing applications is shown in Fig. 1 . Concretely, 3 mL of the as-prepared dots was transferred to a 50-mL measuring flask, and ultra-pure water was added up to the mark to attain the working stock of dots.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of NEQDs was calculated in deionized water by comparing it with quinine sulfate (Φ = 0.54, at 350 nm excitation wavelength) in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference standard, adopting the following Equation. (1) [42]:

In general, various concentrations of HC were transferred into 10-mL volumetric flasks containing 1000 µL of BRB (pH 7) and 35 µL of NEQDs. Next, ultra-pure water was added to the mark and mixed well. The emission intensity at 450 nm was measured upon excitation at 340 nm. A linear calibration plot was obtained after plotting the fluorescence ratio (F0/F) of NEQDs vs. concentrations of HC (µg/mL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of HC in pharmaceutical samples
ce:section.ce:para: Five ampoules of each of Depovit B12® and Depofort B12® were separately mixed, then a certain volume was transferred into a 25-mL measuring flask, then ultra-pure water was added to the mark to attain a final concentration of 100.0 µg/mL. Subsequently, serial concentrations of HC were prepared and measured as indicated previously in Section “2.5”.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of HC in environmental water
ce:section.ce:para: All water samples (river and sewage water) were investigated to determine their ability to estimate and detect HC. In detail, a glass bottle was utilized to collect river water samples 2 m from the shore and 50 cm down from the river surface. The bottle was closed below the surface to ensure that the composition and characteristics of the water did not change. Sewage water samples were collected from the water treatment unit in Mansoura in a glass bottle. The collected samples were centrifuged for 20 min at 6000 rpm and filtered to remove any possible suspended particles. Then, various increasing concentrations of HC were spiked into the samples. Further steps were followed, as provided in Section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of HC in food samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the practicability of this study, we used the developed sensor to conduct spiked recovery experiments on HC in milk and liver samples. Food samples were obtained from a local market in Mansoura, Egypt. Milk samples (1 mL) were transferred into 15-mL centrifugation tubes, and then increasing concentrations of HC were spiked into each one. The tubes were vortex mixed for 2 min, then completed up to 5 mL with methanol to precipitate the protein. The mixture was subjected to centrifugation for 20 min at 4800 rpm. The supernatant was exposed to filtration using a 0.45 μm membrane. Then, 1 mL aliquots of the filtrate were withdrawn and transferred into 10 mL volumetric flasks. The steps previously mentioned under Section 2.5. were followed to test the presence of HC.

Liver samples were homogenized, then a 0.5 g sample was accurately weighed and transferred into 15-mL centrifugation tubes and spiked with different concentrations of HC. After vortex-mixing, methanol was added up to 5 mL, and then the mixture was subjected to centrifugation for 20 min at 4800 rpm. Further, the resultant supernatant was completely filtered using a 0.45 μm membrane filter, and 1 mL aliquots were added to measuring flasks (10 mL). The procedure described under Section 2.5 was performed. The recovery % and relative standard deviation (RSD) were calculated to verify the feasibility of the method for detecting HC in real food products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and morphological characterization of NEQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Our research group has been focusing on quinones as a versatile platform for analytical applications [43–47]. Herein, we utilized NQS and EA as substrates for the immediate synthesis of NEQDs at room temperature. The general class of quinones is very important because their compounds exhibit biological activities against various pathogens associated with important diseases and are used to produce special substances. Among them is NQS, a chemical reagent operated as a derivatizing agent to measure levels of amines and amino acids [48]. NQS is characterized by its availability, ease of manufacture by traditional methods, and applicability. The reagent reacts with them in an alkaline solution to produce a fluorescent material that can be easily detected [49]. EA has numerous significant industrial uses, such as being a “scrubber” to remove CO2, H2S, and other acidic pollutants from waste gas streams. It also acts as a raw material for the fabrication of chelating agents, surfactants, and even pharmaceuticals and as an additive to control the pH of industrial waterways [50]. Precursor selection was performed to ensure that an immediate Schiff’s base polymerization reaction between the carbonyl group (NQS) and an amine (EA) occurred in a suitable state. Initially, the highest QY was sought by manufacturing NEQDs at room temperature by mixing different ratios of the reaction precursors. NEQDs were prepared with different ratios of NQS and EA 1:10, 1:20, 1:40, 1:80, and 1:100, and the QY was calculated in each case. It was observed that when the ratio of EA contribution increased, it led to an increase in the quantum yield, and when the ratio reached 1:100, the QY began to decrease, as shown in Fig. S1A. The substantial changes observed in both the intensity and broadness of peaks with different manufacturing rates are illustrated in Fig. S1B. At the end, the dots used in the study were manufactured based on the highest QY using a 1:80 ratio of NQS: EA.

The morphology of the prepared dots was characterized by operating high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM). As indicated in Fig. 2 A, the CDs displayed a regular distribution ofnanoparticles characterized by the absence of lattice structure, indicating their amorphous nature [51]. Most of the nanoparticles have sizes between 1.0 and 3.0 nm with average size of 1.80 nm (Fig. S2). The elemental compositions of NEQDs are verified by the Energy-Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) and Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM). From Fig. 2B and C,the elemental composition of NEQDs was carbon, oxygen, sulfur, and nitrogen. The functional groups of NEQDs were investigated using FT-IR.As illustrated in Fig. 2D, the spectra exhibited intense peaks at 3400 cm−1 (O–H/N–H stretching), 2932 cm−1 (C–H stretching), 1590 cm−1 (C=C stretching), 1388 cm−1 (O–H bending), 1450 cm−1 (C=O stretching), 2100 cm−1 (C–N band), 1317 cm−1 (C–S stretching), 1650 cm−1 (–C=N) and 555 cm−1 (C–H bending), indicating that the functional groups of NEQDs are hydroxyl and amino groups [52,53].

Moreover, XPS spectra were performed to further investigate the surface groups of NEQDs. XPS survey scan displays the peaks of C1s (286.43 ev), O1s (533.27 ev), N1s (401.08 ev), S2p (169.36 ev), and Na1s (1073.16ev), confirming successful doping with N and S, as indicated in Fig. 2E. The C1s spectrum (Fig. 2F) exhibits three main signals at 285.35 eV (C=C), 289.32 eV (C=N/C=O), and 286.43 eV (C–O/C–S/C–N/–N=C=S) [54]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2G) displays two signals at 399.85 eV (C=N) and 401.71 eV (C–NH2) [55]. The O1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2H) has two peaks at 531.67 eV, and 532.77 eV, corresponding to C=O and O-H, respectively [56]. As presented in Fig. 2I, the S2p spectrum shows two peaks at 167.8 eV and 168.6 eV, which refer to S2p3/2 and S2p1/2 of C-SOx, respectively [57]. Hence, results from the XPS were in agreement with the FT-IR studies of NEQDs.

The yellowish-brown solution of NEQDs exhibited greenish-blue fluorescence emission under ultraviolet (365 nm), as depicted in Fig. 3 A. The maximum emission of NEQDs was observed at 450 nm by excitation at 340 nm. As revealed in Fig. 3B, the emission peaks of the NEQDs solution all occurred at 450 nm when the excitation wavelength changes from 300 to 400 nm, demonstrating excitation-independent emission, which means NEQDs have a single emission center. The excitation-independent phenomenon property specifies that the fluorescence origin of NEQDs is the molecular state [58]. This interesting property of NEQDs can also be ascribed to their well-defined electronic structure, uniform nanoscale dimensions, and constant energy band gap of these dots across this excitation range. Furthermore, the stability of this emission behavior may be revealed for surface states or defect sites [59,60]. The QY of NEQDs was found to be 36.4 %, which exceeded the previously reported values for CDs synthesized at room temperature in the literature, as indicated in Table 1 . Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of the prepared NEQDs was investigated when the concentration of NaCl was raised from 0 to 2.5 M. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, almost no change in fluorescence intensity was observed, confirming the excellent fluorescence stability of NEQDs at high-salt circumstances (2.5 M NaCl). Finally, the effect of storage on the fluorescence of NEQDs was examined. It has been found to be stable for at least 2 months if kept refrigerated at 4 °C in an amber bottle protected from light (Fig. 3D).

Under the described optimum conditions, increasing concentrations of HC (0.30–30.0 μg/mL) were added to the fluorescence system (NEQDs).Upon the addition of HC, the emission intensity of NEQDs at 450 nm was effectively decreased and exhibited a typical concentration-dependent quenching characteristic (Fig. 4 A). Furthermore, the fluorescence ratio (F0/F) was established to have a good linear relationship (r = 0.9995) with HC concentration (μg/mL). (Fig. 4B).

The fluorescence ratio (F0/F) was considered the standard for optimizing the experimental variables, such as the volume of added NEQDs solution, different pH levels, and incubation times. Initially, the impact of the volume of NEQDs solution was studied, and it was found that there were no considerable changes in the F0/F ratio, as indicated in Fig. S3A. Therefore, 35 µL was chosen as an ideal volume of NEQDs for additional studies. Additionally, pH value was a fundamental factor regarding sensitivity, and therefore, BRB was used in the range from 3 to 11. At pH higher than pKa, HC bears a negative charge at its hydroxide and phosphate groups, making it repulsive with the negative NEQDs reducing the static interaction between them, while at lower pH values than pKa, the absorbance of the drug decreases [61], and this decreases inner filter effect (IFE), as indicated in Fig. S3B. Lastly, it was deduced that after adding HC to the prepared dots, the highest quenching occurs at pH 7. Hence, the volume of added buffer was studied to select the optimum one, leading to an effective decrease in FI. Accordingly, 1 mL of pH 7 was the optimal added volume, as illustrated in Fig. S3C. Moreover, the reaction time was examined, and it was found that the observed Fo/F ratio remained consistent with further incubation from 1 min to 25 min, as indicated in Fig. S3D. Therefore, the optimal conditions for this fluorescence “off” sensor were determined to be 35 µL of NEQDs, 1-min incubation in a buffer solution at pH = 7 (1 mL), conducted at room temperature to achieve fast determination.

The mechanism of HC quenching of NEQDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3A, a significant overlap between the UV–vis absorbance spectrum of HC and the excitation and emission spectra of NEQDs can be noticed. Based on these findings, the quenching of NEQDs by HC may be related to IFE or Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). Consequently, the intensity of NEQDs after the addition of HC was corrected using the following equation [11,62,63]:

Fobs stands for the observed emission intensity, while Fcorr describes the emission intensity after IFE correction. Aex and Aem denote the absorbance values at 340 and 450 nm, respectively.

A plot was made between %E vs. HC concentration (µg/mL) (Fig. 4C). Evidently, %E is mostly lost for Fcorr and %E of observed increases, proving that IFE increases with the increase of HC concentration, confirming the occurrence of IFE mechanism. From Fig. 4C, it was deduced that IFE is the main cause of the observed fluorescence variations in NEQDs. Stern Volmer equation (Eq. (4)) was utilized to clarify and understand the other quenching mechanisms at increasing temperatures (302, 313, and 323oK).

The fluorescence of the prepared dots in the presence and absence of HC can be defined by F and F0, respectively. [Q] refers to the concentration of HC,
τ 0
stands to the average lifetime (10−8 s), while Ksv and Kq refer to the Stern-Volmer and quenching rate constants, respectively. From Fig. 4D, the detected decrease in Ksv with increasing temperature (302, 313, and 323oK) points out a static quenching effect (SQE) controlling the reaction process. In addition, the absorption spectra of NEQDs were measured in the absence and presence of HC. As illustrated in Fig. S4, a new absorption maximum appears when HC is added to the NEQDs solution, representing the formation of a ground-state complex with the fluorophore. To further explain the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, the fluorescence decay kinetics of NEQDs have been studied through time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) in the presence of HC. The average lifetime values in the absence and presence of HC were calculated to be 1.52 and 1.34 ns, respectively. As seen in Fig. 4E, the lifetime data display no obvious change in fluorescent lifetime upon the addition of HC (quencher), which confirms that the quenching is caused by a static quenching process [64]. These previous findings confirmed that the quenching mechanism involved was both IFE and SQE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the content and results of the analytical methodology
Linearity and limit of detection and quantitation
ce:section.ce:para: The designed system was evaluated and assessed as per the guidelines for the validation of analytical approaches according to ICH recommendations [65,66]. The documented values of the F0/F ratio demonstrated excellent linearity across HC concentrations of 0.30–30.0 µg /mL, as indicated in Fig. 4A and B. The linear regression equation was assessed to be F0/F ratio = 0.048·HC + 1.015 (r = 0.9995), Tables S1, S2 and S3. The quantitation limit (LOQ = 10 Sa/b) and the detection limit (LOD, 3.3 Sa /b) were calculated to be 0.30 µg /mL and 0.1 µg/mL, respectively, where Sa refers to the standard deviation of the blank response and the calibration curve's slope was denoted by the variable b.

The satisfactory performance of the developed sensor indicates its great potential for HC estimation in different water and food samples. In addition, the test study avoids the use of expensive devices, reagents, and complex steps, which greatly simplifies the manufacturing procedure of NEQDs and reduces costs. Overall, the proposed method is a competitive and promising probe in HC detection. The method type, linear ranges, and LODs of previously reported strategies can be seen in Table 2 . Some methods have shown higher sensitivity than our designed system but suffer from the use of laborious probe fabrication, complex instrumentation, tiresome pre-concentrating steps, and the requirement of specially trained analysts [28,32,33].

To assess and determine the precision of the developed methodology, intra-day precision was evaluated by measuring three replicates at three diverse concentration levels covering the high, medium, and low ranges of the calibration curve on the same day. Moreover, inter-day precision was assessed over three subsequent days. Method precision was stated as RSD%, where RSD% values did not exceed 2.0 %, indicating that the designed method shows satisfactory accuracy Table S4.

The selectivity of the NEQDs system for HC detection was investigated by testing a variety of potentially interfering substances. These interfering agents include lactose, glucose, SO3 2−, SO4 2−, S2O3 2−, Cl−, CH3COO−, K+, Na+, Pb2+, Ba2+ Fe2+, Fe3+, and different amino acids (l-cysteine, l-tryptophan, l-lysine, l-histidine, and l-threonine) all at concentrations 50 times higher than HC concentration. Additionally, some co-administered and co-formulated drugs, including l-carnitine, thiamine (vitamin B1), and biotin (vitamin B7), have been added to NEQDs in the presence of HC. The results in Fig. 5 prove the extremely selective response of this sensing probe to HC over other interfering substances. It is clear that even at high concentrations, these substances did not interfere with HC estimation. The tolerance limit was examined in detail in Supplementary Section S2. The reason for the high selectivity of the synthesized NEQDs towards HC is that the quenching of fluorescence occurs due to the strong inner filter quenching effect (IFE) of HC on NEQDs fluorescence as HC has a wide UV–visible spectrum that intersects with both the excitation and emission wavelengths of NEQDs. In addition, our investigations using the Stern Volmer equation demonstrated that a static non-fluorescence complex occurs between HC and NEQDs, leading to a synergistic quenching effect with the IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of HC in pharmaceutical ampoules
ce:section.ce:para: Our proposed methodology was employed to determine HC in its pharmaceutical dosage forms, specifically Depovit B12® and Depofort B12® ampoules. As illustrated in Table S5, the designed system was subjected to evaluation, revealing a satisfactory recovery ratio and, thus, reliable and accurate estimation of HC content. The absence of any possible interference from other ingredients present in the commercial forms was confirmed by the high recovery percentage obtained. For additional evaluation, the official BP spectrophotometric method [67] was applied for comparison with our developed study via t-test and F-test (Table S5), and there was no significant difference between our method and the official one. Therefore, this method was appropriate for the estimation of HC in commercial samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay of HC in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Hydroxocobalamin is commonly considered safe and well-tolerated. However, over the last several years, the administration of HC has led to the development of acute kidney injury [68]. Additionally, HC is excreted in urine and, in turn, released in wastewater. Human and veterinary drugs are constantly released into the environmental waterways as a result of industrial processes, metabolic excretion, or inappropriate disposal [69]. Hence, it is necessary to investigate the reliability and practicability of NEQDs for sensing HC in real water samples. The proposed methodology was set to assay the amount of HC in sewage water and River Nile water samples. As demonstrated in Table 3 , the values of RSD% ranged from 1.15 to 1.45 %, and the recoveries ranged from 98.90 % to 101.60 % for river and sewage water. Thus, this study was appropriate for detecting HC in real water samples.

ce:section-title.ce:italic: Assay of HC in food samples

ce:section.ce:para: The applicability of the designed study to detect HC in milk and liver samples was investigated. The analytical results for real samples spiked with standard HC concentration are summarized in Table 3, demonstrating that %recovery values range from 98.00 % to 102.09 % and RSD% of less than 3 % (2.21 % to 2.40 %). Hence, the accuracy and consistency of the sensor for HC estimation in pharmaceutical applications were confirmed (Table 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: System greenness, blueness, and whiteness evaluation
ce:section.ce:para: Numerous factors were taken into consideration to evaluate the greenness of the developed methodology for HC quantitation using NEQDs. These factors include the use of the as-prepared dots, a sustainable and green material, and slight usage of energy and reagents in the assay process. Greenness is assessed by applying more than one greenness assessment tool where harmonious and synergistic results can be achieved. Firstly, the complex green analytical procedure index (Complex-GAPI) is an established software that evaluates the entire methodology from sample collection to the final assay step. An additional hexagonal shape that represents the pre-analysis steps is included in the GAPI assessment tool. The environmental impact of this assessment is shown on a color scale; red indicates a high-risk effect, while green and yellow represent a medium and low effect, respectively. All used reagents, solvents, and generated waste are calculated via E-factor [70,71], as illustrated in Fig. S5A.

Additionally, AGREEprep was mainly performed to investigate the greenness of the sample preparation.It is a circular pictogram with a score value in the middle ranging from 0 to 1. The higher the score, the greener the method is. Typically, it consists of ten main sections with different lengths, representing the greenness principles. The length is related to the assigned weight [72,73]. The greenness of the designed methodology is demonstrated by the green center of the pictogram with a value of 0.92, as shown in Fig. S5B.

Moreover, the AGREE approach was applied to provide a descriptive and numerical value for the assay of the degree of eco-friendliness of the analytical procedures. It is a clock-shaped graph consisting of 12 parts that reflect 12 principles for evaluating greenness. The color contrast of red, yellow, and green pictograms indicates low, medium, and high greenness values [74]. The AGREE tool showed that the proposed method has a significant greenness with an overall score of 0.80 (Fig. S5C), confirming its eco-friendly nature. In summary, the greenness of our method is related to minimal sample preparation stages, absence of derivatization steps, minimal reagent usage, low power requirements of the spectrofluorimeter, and high throughput of the analytical process.

The blue applicability grade index (BAGI) of the method indicates its potential effect on analytical chemistry and its applicability to a wide and comprehensive range of applications. BAGI represents a complement to established green standards and focuses primarily on the practical aspects of White Analytical Chemistry (WAC). It assesses ten key attributes, and the overall score ranges from 25 to 100 in the inner section of the pictogram. BAGI permits a comprehensive evaluation of the blueness or applicability of an analytical method according to ten main parameters: instrumentation, analysis type, sample preparation needs, number of analytes, sample throughput, preconcentration needs, degree of automation, materials/reagents needed, samples investigated per hour and amount of sample required. The worst performance of method applicability is indicated by a score value of 25 with white color, while a score value of 100 represents the outstanding performance of the method with dark blue color [75]. The findings of this evaluation showed a blue applicability score index for the designed system of 70 (Fig. S5D), reflecting its operative influence and wide applicability in analytical chemistry.

Furthermore, WAC is a complementary assessment tool applied in analytical approaches, which is an extension of greenness principles. Whiteness assessment consists of three areas, including blue (economic practical aspects), green (environmental friendliness and safety), and red (analytical effectiveness), which complement each other and, after mixing, give a white color. This white color reflects a well-balanced, applicable, and sustainable analytical method. The four-red principles are assigned to method sensitivity, accuracy, precision, applicability, and resistance to potential interference. The green principles (G1-G4) represent the degree of friendliness, energy consumption, and toxicity of the reagents, while the blue principles (B1-B4) refer to the degree of method simplicity, time and cost efficiency, and applicability [76]. The overall score is obtained from the submission of the score of the three domains. The score mainly ranges from 0 (the worst performance) to 100 (the ideal performance) [77]. The designed study shows a high fit to WAC based on a score of 96.8 % over other reported techniques for the assay of HC in the field of economic aspects, environmental friendliness, and analytical effectiveness, as indicated in Table 1 and Fig. S5E.

An optimized new and rapid method for the preparation of highly fluorescent NEQDs at room temperature was developed. The well-functionalized NEQDs produced visible greenish-blue fluorescence with strong excitation-independent emission at 450 nm and a superior QY of 36.40 %, which is higher than many of the previously reported QDs. Under optimal parameters, the fluorescence intensity of NEQDs after HC addition decreased within the range of 0.30–30.0 μg/mL at 450 nm via IFE and SQE. NEQDs were applied as a “turn-off” fluorescent sensor for HC with a detection limit of 0.1 μg/mL. Consequently, the successful application of NEQDs as a fluorescent sensor has enabled the selective measurement of HC in commercial ampoules and trace amounts in real water samples, as well as food samples, with a good recovery of 98.0 %–102.9 %. This study incorporates the high value-added property of NEQDs preparation at room temperature, enlarges their application range, and provides a cost-effective, green, and large-scale system for CD synthesis.
